stem: A 9-year-old girl admitted with severe DKA (pH 7.05, BSL 32, ketones 5.6) was rehydrated with 0.9 percent sodium chloride at 5 mL/kg/h plus an insulin infusion at 0.05 unit/kg/h. Five hours into treatment she develops new-onset confusion and a fronto-occipital headache. Her parents report she 'doesn't recognise them' and she has had one episode of vomiting in the last 30 minutes. The bedside nurse notes that her BP has risen from 110/64 to 138/86 and her heart rate has fallen from 124 to 78.

data_table.Examination: Pupils 3 mm equal reactive, no focal weakness, bilateral plantar flexion responses; abdomen soft
data_table.Investigations: BSL 14 mmol/L, ketones 3.2, pH 7.20 (improving), Na 140, K 4.1, corrected Na rising from 134 to 140

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Continue current fluid and insulin rates as her acidosis is improving and reassess in 30 minutes
options.rationale: Cushing triad (bradycardia, hypertension, irregular respiration) plus altered mental status during DKA treatment is presumed cerebral oedema requiring immediate action. (Source: RCH CPG: DKA; ISPAD 2022)

options.text: Increase the insulin infusion rate to 0.1 unit/kg/h to reduce hyperglycaemia more aggressively
options.rationale: Increasing insulin worsens cerebral oedema by accelerating osmotic shifts; the priority is treating raised ICP. (Source: ISPAD DKA 2022)

options.text: Halve the fluid rate, give hypertonic saline 3 percent 3 mL/kg over 10 minutes and elevate head
options.rationale: Suspected cerebral oedema in paediatric DKA is treated with immediate fluid reduction and hypertonic 3 percent saline 2.5 to 5 mL/kg (or mannitol 0.5 to 1 g/kg) plus 30 degree head-up positioning. (Source: RCH CPG: DKA; ISPAD 2022)

options.text: Administer sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg bolus to correct the residual acidosis quickly
options.rationale: Bicarbonate worsens cerebral oedema risk and is not indicated in paediatric DKA except for severe hyperkalaemia with cardiac compromise. (Source: ISPAD DKA 2022)

options.text: Perform urgent CT brain before any intervention to confirm cerebral oedema is the cause now
options.rationale: Cerebral oedema in DKA is a clinical diagnosis and treatment must precede imaging; delays for CT worsen outcome. (Source: RCH CPG: DKA)

explanation.summary: Cerebral oedema complicates 0.5 to 1 percent of paediatric DKA and accounts for the majority of DKA mortality. Risk factors: under 5 years, new-onset diabetes, severe acidosis (pH under 7.1), elevated urea, low pCO2, high-volume fluid administration in first 4 hours, bicarbonate use. Warning signs: headache, altered mental state, Cushing triad, focal neurology, falling sodium during treatment. Treatment is immediate hypertonic saline 3 percent (2.5 to 5 mL/kg) OR mannitol 0.5 to 1 g/kg, halving fluid rate, 30 degree head-up. CT after stabilisation.
key_points: Cerebral oedema in 0.5 to 1 percent of paeds DKA; majority of DKA mortality.
Cushing triad + altered mental state = clinical diagnosis.
Treatment: hypertonic 3 percent NaCl 2.5 to 5 mL/kg OR mannitol 0.5 to 1 g/kg.
Halve fluid rate, elevate head 30 degrees, secure airway if GCS drops.
Risk factors: under 5, new-onset, pH under 7.1, raised urea, low pCO2.
explanation.pearls: Failure of serum sodium to rise as glucose falls (Hillier corrected sodium not increasing) during DKA treatment is the single best biochemical warning of impending cerebral oedema and warrants slowing fluid rate even before clinical features appear.

stem: A 6-year-old boy is reviewed in clinic 3 weeks after diagnosis with Henoch-Schonlein purpura (now IgA vasculitis) following a URTI. He had palpable purpura over both lower limbs and buttocks plus right ankle arthritis. The rash and joint symptoms have resolved. He has no abdominal pain, no haematuria visible and his BP at this visit is 102/68. His parents ask what the follow-up plan should be.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 102/68 (50th centile for age and height), HR 88, T 36.5°C
data_table.Examination: Fading petechiae on lower legs, full range of joint movement, abdomen soft non-tender
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis at diagnosis: 2+ blood, no protein; serum creatinine 38 micromol/L; albumin 38 g/L; today's urinalysis: 1+ blood, no protein

lead_in: Which monitoring plan is most appropriate?

options.text: Discharge from follow-up as rash has resolved and renal involvement appears self-limited
options.rationale: Up to 50 percent of HSP nephritis appears in the first 3 months and a small fraction progresses to CKD; weekly to fortnightly urinalysis and BP monitoring for 6 months is required. (Source: KHA-CARI HSP 2023; RCH CPG)

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 4 weeks to prevent late nephritis as prophylaxis
options.rationale: Corticosteroids do not prevent late nephritis in HSP; they are reserved for severe abdominal pain, scrotal involvement, or biopsy-proven nephritis. (Source: KHA-CARI HSP 2023)

options.text: Weekly urinalysis plus BP for 6 weeks then monthly for 6 months and nephrology referral if abnormal
options.rationale: AU and KHA-CARI guidance is weekly urinalysis and BP for 4 to 6 weeks, then monthly to 6 months, referring if persistent proteinuria, declining renal function or hypertension develops. (Source: KHA-CARI HSP 2023; RCH CPG)

options.text: Arrange immediate renal biopsy to assess crescentic disease prior to immunosuppressive therapy
options.rationale: Renal biopsy is reserved for nephrotic-range proteinuria, declining GFR, or persistent abnormal urinalysis - none currently present. (Source: KHA-CARI HSP 2023)

options.text: Single urinalysis and BP check at 12 months as he is now asymptomatic and well-appearing
options.rationale: Late-presenting nephritis would be missed by such infrequent monitoring; 6 months of structured surveillance is standard. (Source: KHA-CARI HSP 2023)

explanation.summary: IgA vasculitis (formerly HSP) is the commonest small-vessel vasculitis in children, presenting with palpable purpura, arthritis or arthralgia, abdominal pain, and IgA nephritis. Renal involvement appears in around 40 percent (mostly within 6 weeks, occasionally up to 6 months after diagnosis); 1 to 2 percent progress to CKD. AU monitoring is weekly urinalysis (dipstick) and BP for 4 to 6 weeks, monthly to 6 months. Refer if persistent proteinuria, declining renal function, hypertension, or nephrotic features. Corticosteroids treat severe abdominal pain or scrotal involvement but do not prevent late nephritis.
key_points: Renal involvement in around 40 percent; 1 to 2 percent CKD.
Monitor weekly urinalysis + BP for 4 to 6 weeks, monthly to 6 months.
Refer for persistent proteinuria, declining GFR, hypertension, nephrosis.
Steroids treat severe pain/scrotal, NOT for renal prevention.
Renal biopsy if nephrotic, declining GFR, or persistent abnormalities.
explanation.pearls: Intussusception complicates around 2 percent of paediatric HSP and is typically ileo-ileal (not ileo-colic) - air enema may miss it; consider ultrasound first in any HSP child with severe abdominal pain regardless of stool patterns.

stem: A 7-year-old girl presents with 36 hours of right eye swelling that began after a URTI. The swelling has progressed and she now reports double vision when looking to the left. She has not been able to fully open the right eye since this morning. There is no history of trauma or insect bite. She is up to date with immunisations including Hib.

data_table.Examination: Right eye: marked periorbital erythema and oedema, proptosis 4 mm by exophthalmometer, painful restricted eye movements (failure to abduct), diplopia on left gaze; visual acuity 6/9 right, 6/6 left
data_table.Investigations: WCC 18 x10^9/L, neutrophils 14, CRP 168 mg/L; blood culture pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Oral cephalexin 25 mg/kg three times daily with planned emergency department review in 24 hours
options.rationale: Proptosis, ophthalmoplegia and diplopia are post-septal (orbital) cellulitis until proved otherwise; oral monotherapy is inadequate. (Source: eTG; RCH CPG: Periorbital and orbital cellulitis)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Periorbital and orbital cellulitis

options.text: Topical chloramphenicol eye drops with oral amoxicillin-clavulanate twice daily for 5 days
options.rationale: Topical antibiotics treat conjunctivitis; established orbital infection requires parenteral antibiotics and imaging. (Source: eTG)

options.text: Admit for urgent CT of orbits and sinuses and commence IV ceftriaxone plus flucloxacillin
options.rationale: Orbital (post-septal) cellulitis is suggested by proptosis, painful EOM, diplopia or visual loss; AU first-line is urgent CT and IV ceftriaxone plus flucloxacillin plus ENT and ophthalmology review. (Source: eTG; RCH CPG)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Periorbital and orbital cellulitis

options.text: Discharge home with daily GP review and structured safety-netting advice on deterioration
options.rationale: Orbital cellulitis can progress to cavernous sinus thrombosis or vision loss within hours; outpatient management is unsafe. (Source: RCH CPG)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Periorbital and orbital cellulitis

options.text: Perform emergency lateral canthotomy and cantholysis prior to any imaging or antibiotic
options.rationale: Canthotomy is for orbital compartment syndrome (retrobulbar haemorrhage), not orbital cellulitis; imaging guides surgical drainage if a subperiosteal abscess is present. (Source: RCH CPG)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Periorbital and orbital cellulitis

explanation.summary: Periorbital (pre-septal) cellulitis is anterior to the orbital septum, presenting with eyelid erythema and oedema, normal eye movements, no proptosis, no visual loss. Orbital (post-septal) cellulitis - typically a complication of acute ethmoiditis - presents with the additional findings of proptosis, painful restricted eye movements, diplopia and visual changes. Orbital cellulitis demands urgent CT of orbits and paranasal sinuses, IV ceftriaxone plus flucloxacillin (covering S. pyogenes, S. aureus and sinus organisms), ophthalmology and ENT involvement, and surgical drainage if a subperiosteal abscess is large or visual function declines.
key_points: Pre-septal: eyelid erythema, normal eye movements - oral antibiotics OK.
Post-septal: proptosis, EOM restriction, diplopia, vision change - emergency.
Workup: CT orbits and sinuses with contrast.
AU antibiotics: IV ceftriaxone + flucloxacillin (paeds dosing).
Surgical drainage for medium-large subperiosteal abscess or visual decline.
explanation.pearls: Visual acuity, pupillary reactions and colour vision (red desaturation test) are the most sensitive bedside markers of optic nerve compromise in orbital cellulitis - re-check them every hour during the first 24 hours of treatment to detect occult deterioration.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Periorbital and orbital cellulitis

stem: A breastfed term male newborn (gestation 39+2 weeks, birthweight 3.4 kg) is reviewed on day 4 with visible jaundice noted by the midwife at home visit. He is feeding 8 times daily and producing 5 to 6 wet nappies. His mother is blood group O Rh-positive; cord blood and DAT are negative. He has had no previous transcutaneous bilirubin recorded. He is alert and feeding actively in the clinic.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8°C, HR 138, RR 42; alert, well-perfused, weight 3.28 kg (-3.5 percent from birth)
data_table.Examination: Jaundice to chest and proximal limbs, soft fontanelle, no hepatosplenomegaly, no petechiae, normal Moro and tone
data_table.Investigations: Total serum bilirubin 320 micromol/L, direct bilirubin 8 micromol/L (well under 20 percent), Hb 168 g/L, retics 2.5 percent, blood film normal

options.text: Admit for phototherapy with continued breastfeeding and review bilirubin in 6 hours
options.rationale: Bilirubin above the AU phototherapy line at 96 hours requires admission for phototherapy with continued breastfeeding plus a 6-hour repeat bilirubin to assess response. (Source: ANZ neonatal jaundice consensus 2024)

options.text: Reassure parents and review with repeat transcutaneous bilirubin in 48 hours
options.rationale: Total bilirubin 320 micromol/L at 96 hours in a term low-risk infant exceeds the AU phototherapy line; outpatient deferral risks kernicterus. (Source: ANZ neonatal jaundice consensus 2024; RWH paediatric jaundice)

options.text: Cease breastfeeding and switch to formula for 48 hours to confirm breast milk jaundice
options.rationale: Routine cessation of breastfeeding is not recommended; AU first-line is phototherapy with continued breastfeeding and lactation support. (Source: ANZ consensus 2024)

options.text: Arrange urgent double-volume exchange transfusion as bilirubin exceeds the exchange threshold today
options.rationale: Exchange transfusion threshold at 96 hours in a low-risk term infant is around 430 to 450 micromol/L; 320 is in the phototherapy not exchange range. (Source: ANZ consensus 2024)

options.text: Commence oral phenobarbitone 5 mg/kg/day to induce bilirubin conjugation enzymes safely
options.rationale: Phenobarbitone is not standard for neonatal jaundice; phototherapy is first-line and rapidly effective. (Source: ANZ consensus 2024)

explanation.summary: Neonatal jaundice management in AU uses age-specific phototherapy and exchange transfusion nomograms (eg KEMH/RWH/ANZ consensus 2024). Risk factors raise the action threshold lower: under 38 weeks, haemolysis (positive DAT, ABO/Rh mismatch, G6PD), sepsis, hypoalbuminaemia. Phototherapy is given with eye protection, breastfeeding continued (or expressed milk if too sleepy to feed), with 6-hourly bilirubin once treatment commences. Stop phototherapy when bilirubin falls 50 micromol/L below the line. Direct bilirubin over 20 percent of total prompts conjugated-hyperbilirubinaemia workup (biliary atresia, sepsis, metabolic).
key_points: Use age-specific phototherapy and exchange nomograms.
Lower threshold for risk factors: prematurity, haemolysis, sepsis, hypoalbuminaemia.
Continue breastfeeding during phototherapy; eye protection.
Recheck bilirubin at 6 hours then 6 to 12 hourly to confirm response.
Conjugated fraction > 20 percent of total = workup for cholestasis.
explanation.pearls: Conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia at any age beyond day 14 in a term infant is biliary atresia until proved otherwise - delay in Kasai portoenterostomy beyond 60 days of life roughly halves the chance of long-term liver-graft-free survival.

subtopic_detail: Adrenal crisis on chronic steroid replacement
stem: A 56-year-old woman with Addison's disease on hydrocortisone 10 mg morning and 5 mg afternoon plus fludrocortisone 100 mcg daily is brought to ED by her partner. She has had 24 hours of fevers, cough and progressive lethargy. This morning she vomited her hydrocortisone dose. On arrival she is hypotensive and confused. Her medic-alert bracelet identifies the diagnosis.

data_table.Examination: Confused (GCS 14), warm peripheries, RR 26, no neck stiffness, right basal crackles; capillary refill 3 s
data_table.Investigations: Na 128 mmol/L, K 5.6 mmol/L, glucose 3.4 mmol/L, urea 9.2, creatinine 124, lactate 3.8, CRP 198 mg/L; CXR right lower lobe consolidation

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Double her usual oral hydrocortisone dose and observe in ED with intravenous saline overnight
options.rationale: Oral dosing is inadequate when vomiting and shocked; IV hydrocortisone is required. (Source: ESE 2020)

options.text: Intravenous hydrocortisone 100 mg stat plus crystalloid resuscitation and empirical antibiotics
options.rationale: Adrenal crisis (hypotension, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia) on top of sepsis requires immediate IV hydrocortisone 100 mg, IV fluid resuscitation, glucose correction and antibiotics per sepsis pathway. (Source: ESE adrenal crisis 2020; eTG)

options.text: Start IV dexamethasone 4 mg with stim test scheduled before any glucocorticoid replacement therapy
options.rationale: Diagnosis is already established by medic-alert bracelet and clinical picture; do not delay hydrocortisone for biochemical testing in established Addison's. (Source: ESE 2020)

options.text: Administer noradrenaline infusion targeting MAP 65 before any steroid replacement is given
options.rationale: Vasopressors are ineffective without glucocorticoid replacement in established adrenal crisis; hydrocortisone must come first. (Source: ESE 2020)

options.text: Withhold steroid dosing pending confirmation of sepsis source by chest CT before treatment starts
options.rationale: Delay for imaging in shocked adrenal crisis worsens outcome; treat empirically and image after stabilisation. (Source: ESE 2020)

explanation.summary: Adrenal crisis is a medical emergency in patients with primary or secondary adrenal insufficiency, precipitated by infection, surgery, trauma, missed steroid doses, vomiting or stress. Features include hypotension, shock, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia (primary AI only), hypoglycaemia and altered mental state. Immediate management is IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat (continuing 200 mg/24 h infusion or 50 mg every 6 hours), IV isotonic saline resuscitation (1 L in first hour), glucose correction, and empirical treatment of the precipitant. Educate patients on sick-day rules: double oral dose for fevers/minor illness, IM hydrocortisone (Solu-Cortef) injection for vomiting.
key_points: Adrenal crisis = hypotension + hyponatraemia + hyperkalaemia + hypoglycaemia.
Immediate IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat.
IV crystalloid 1 L in first hour, then maintenance.
Treat precipitant (sepsis): empirical antibiotics per Therapeutic Guidelines.
Patient education: double oral dose for minor illness; IM hydrocortisone for vomiting.
explanation.pearls: Patients on regular prednisolone 5 mg or higher for 4 weeks or longer have functional secondary adrenal insufficiency and need stress-dose hydrocortisone for major surgery or sepsis even if their primary disease (eg PMR, asthma) seems quiescent.
